quote: I looked into the whole thing before, but I didn't like it because of the prices, because of the terms, and a lot of stuff.
stewart_read: This is a qualified re-engagement signal, not just an objection. A customer who looked into solar before, formed specific opinions about prices and terms, waited for the market to mature, and is now back on the phone is a warmer lead than a cold inbound. The rep heard 'prior dissatisfaction' and moved on. The strategic read is that this was the emotional anchor the entire call should have been built around — 'you waited for things to get better, let's see if they did for your home specifically.'
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been deliberately moving past the objection to avoid scope creep into pricing discussions — a setter-scope discipline move. But the codex's verify.interest_in_solar directive calls for 60+ seconds of depth here, and the rep spent fewer than 10 seconds on it. The scope-discipline hypothesis doesn't hold.
coaching_implication: Play this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask the rep: 'What did you hear when he said prices, terms, and confusion?' Then show what a curiosity hook looks like here — something like: 'A lot has changed in the last year — that's actually why I'm calling. Want to see what the numbers look like now for your specific address?' The rep needs to hear that prior skepticism + re-engagement = high-value signal, not a box to check.

quote: Yeah. Yeah. Kinda get a little bit more research through. Yeah. Is this, how long ago was the last time you looked into it?
stewart_read: This is the second pass at the customer's prior-bad-experience signal, and the rep again acknowledged without engaging. The rep's pivot to a timeline question ('how long ago') is a verify-checklist move — it captures data but does not build emotional alignment. The codex's verify.interest_in_solar directive calls for depth and emotional anchoring here. The rep's line 'Times have definitely changed' at 01:17 was the closest thing to a curiosity hook in the call, but it was delivered as a throwaway rather than a genuine pivot. Two touches on the same concern, zero depth on either.
alternative_hypothesis: One reading is that the rep was deliberately deferring emotional engagement until after the bill anchor was established — a sequencing choice. This reading is roughly co-equal with the checklist reading: the call structure does move toward bill collection next, and the rep may have intended to flip the prior-bad-experience concern once the bill figure was in hand. However, the bill figure was already on file from the form ($100–$150 at 01:51), and the rep never returned to the prior-experience concern after collecting it. The deferral-to-bill-anchor reading loses support once the bill is captured and the concern is still not addressed.
coaching_implication: This is the same gap as the 00:41 moment — surface both in the same coaching conversation. The pattern is: rep hears emotional content, acknowledges it, moves to the next verify item. The fix is not a script change; it's a habit change. The rep needs to practice sitting with the customer's stated concern for 20–30 seconds before moving on.

quote: No. It's not that much. No. It's bad.
stewart_read: The customer self-reports a credit score below 670. The codex's qualifiers.credit_qualifier section explicitly flags this as an open policy question — setter-level DQ on stated credit may be killing real deals on unreliable self-reporting. The rep accepted the stated number without any exploration. The strategic read would be that the rep is following the script correctly. The reactive read is that the rep is DQ'ing on a number the customer may be misreporting (Vantage vs FICO, bureau variation, etc.). The codex does not resolve this — it flags it as a question for Kenny. The coaching gap here is not that the rep DQ'd; it's that the rep had no tool to handle the ambiguity.
alternative_hypothesis: If Ion's policy is 'setter DQs on stated credit,' the rep executed correctly. If Ion's policy is 'let the closer's pull decide,' this is a lost lead. The manager needs to resolve this policy question before coaching the rep on this specific moment.
coaching_implication: Do not coach the rep on this moment until the credit-DQ policy is resolved with Kenny (see codex qualifiers.credit_qualifier policy question). Once resolved: if the policy is 'let closer decide,' this call is a coaching gap. If the policy is 'setter DQs on stated,' the rep executed correctly and the coaching moment is elsewhere.

quote: Is that the the same thing with your wife as well? Is it below 670? Yeah. The same thing.
stewart_read: The wife was identified as a co-owner at 01:43 ('Only me and my wife'). The rep correctly noted her presence in the verify section. At 02:25, the rep checks the wife's credit as a secondary qualifier — which is procedurally correct. However, the wife was never engaged as a co-decision-maker in the appointment-setting sense. The codex's protocols.spouse_decision directive requires that a spouse on title be included in the appointment. In this call, the credit DQ made that moot — but the pattern is worth noting: the wife appeared in the verify section as a data point, not as a person whose presence at the appointment needed to be secured.
alternative_hypothesis: Given the call ended in a DQ, the spouse-inclusion protocol was never triggered in a meaningful sense. The rep's handling of the wife as a credit-qualifier check rather than a co-decision-maker is a pattern flag, not a call-specific failure.
coaching_implication: Flag this as a pattern to watch across this rep's calls. The wife was on title — the rep noted it and moved on. If this call had not DQ'd on credit, would the rep have secured the wife's presence at the appointment? Pull a non-DQ call from this rep and check.

quote: Okay. Yeah. Well, unfortunately, then it wouldn't really make sense as of right now.
stewart_read: The rep's DQ delivery is soft and non-committal ('wouldn't really make sense as of right now') — which is appropriate in tone but leaves the door open without a clear path. The phrase 'as of right now' implies a future opportunity but the rep immediately contradicts it in the next line by saying 'I'll take you off our list.' The rep is trying to be kind but the message is incoherent: we'll follow up, but we're removing you. This is a lead-preservation failure regardless of whether the DQ itself was correct.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been following a script instruction to soften the DQ with a future-touch promise. If so, the script itself creates the contradiction — 'follow up in a couple months' and 'take you off our list' cannot coexist.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the contradiction between 02:37 and 02:32. If the intent is to preserve the lead for a future touch, the rep needs a clean close: 'I'll flag your file for a follow-up in a few months — things can change.' If the intent is to DQ cleanly, the rep needs to drop the follow-up promise. The current hybrid does neither.

quote: I'll make a note here to follow-up with you in a couple of months just to see where everything's kind of at, but I'll take you off our list for now.
stewart_read: This is the codex's soft_outcome_distinction failure mode in its clearest form. The rep makes two contradictory commitments in a single sentence: a follow-up promise ('I'll make a note to follow up in a couple of months') and a list removal ('I'll take you off our list for now'). These cannot coexist. A follow-up note with no locked time and an immediate list removal is not a callback — it is a polite exit. The customer has no reason to expect contact, and the rep has no mechanism to deliver it. This is not a conditional booking; it is a soft discard dressed as a future touch.
alternative_hypothesis: The strategic read would be that the rep is preserving the lead in CRM while removing the customer from active outreach — a legitimate pipeline management move. But the rep's phrasing ('take you off our list') signals to the customer that contact is ending, which undermines any future outreach. The strategic read requires the rep to have a real CRM follow-up mechanism, which is not evidenced in the call.
coaching_implication: Play this line in the 1-on-1 and ask the rep: 'What happens to this lead after this call?' If the answer is 'it goes in the CRM for a future touch,' the rep needs to learn to communicate that clearly to the customer — 'I'll flag your file and reach back out in a few months when things may have changed' — without the list-removal language that signals finality. If the answer is 'nothing,' the rep needs to understand that a follow-up promise with no mechanism is a broken promise.

quote: I do wanna just make sure that I was taking a look at the the address there. You have a very beautiful home, gets lots and lots of sunlight, so that's great to see.
stewart_read: The rep uses satellite imagery to deliver a personalized compliment about the home — a micro-trust move that is structurally sound. It signals preparation and specificity, which reduces scam anxiety. However, the compliment is delivered in a slightly breathless, salesperson-adjacent register ('very beautiful home, gets lots and lots of sunlight') that may not match the energy of a 45–70 year old homeowner who has already been burned by solar pitches. The move is right; the delivery is worth watching.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may be executing a deliberate legitimacy-building move from the intro playbook — using the satellite pull to establish credibility. This is the stronger read. The energy-match concern is secondary.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this as a good instinct — using the satellite pull to personalize is exactly right. Coach the delivery: the compliment lands better at a slower pace and lower register. 'I pulled up your address — your roof gets strong sun exposure, which is a good sign' lands more credibly than 'very beautiful home, lots and lots of sunlight.'

quote: Times have definitely changed. I mean, things have gotten a little better, so don't blame me for at least wanting to check back into it.
stewart_read: This is the closest the rep came to a curiosity hook in the entire call. 'Times have definitely changed' is the seed of an objection-inversion move — the customer's prior bad experience becomes the reason to look again now. But the rep delivers it as a throwaway ('don't blame me for at least wanting to check back into it') rather than a genuine pivot. The line needed to land as a question: 'A lot has changed since last year — want to see what the numbers look like now for your specific home?' Instead it reads as a filler acknowledgment before the next verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been intentionally keeping the energy low to avoid triggering the customer's prior-bad-experience skepticism. But the codex's curiosity_over_features directive calls for creating curiosity, not suppressing it. The low-energy delivery here is a missed opportunity, not a strategic choice.
coaching_implication: This line is the rep's best instinct in the call — surface it as a positive. Then show what it looks like when the instinct is fully executed: turn 'times have changed' into a genuine curiosity hook that invites the customer to see their specific numbers. The rep has the right idea; they need to learn to commit to it.
